original_hypothesis.title: emergencyWithdraw Unchecked Underflow Enables Complete Fund Drain
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: emergencyWithdraw calls _processWithdrawal which uses unchecked arithmetic to subtract from balances[user][token] and totalDeposits[token] without any balance validation, zero-amount check, or access restriction. Any user with zero balance can call emergencyWithdraw(token, 1) when the contract holds tokens from other depositors. The unchecked subtraction wraps balances[attacker][token] to type(uint128).max - 0 (approximately 3.4e38). The safeTransfer succeeds because the contract holds tokens. The attacker then calls withdraw() with the full remaining contract balance, passing the balance check since their underflowed balance is astronomically large, draining all funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a straightforward code vulnerability: _processWithdrawal has no balance validation and uses unchecked arithmetic. emergencyWithdraw exposes this to any external caller. The attack requires only that the contract holds tokens from other depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { balances[user][token] -= amount; totalDeposits[token] -= amount; } with no balance check in emergencyWithdraw or _processWithdrawal

preconditions: Contract holds at least 1 unit of the target token from other depositors
Attacker has 0 (or any) balance for the target token

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: balances[user][token] -= amount in unchecked block wraps on underflow; emergencyWithdraw has no checks at all
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance check (balances[user][token] >= amount), no zero-amount check, no access control

analysis_questions: Can the underflowed balance be used to drain via withdraw()?
Does totalDeposits underflow prevent subsequent withdrawals?
Can this be combined with other functions for amplified damage?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract, have victim deposit tokens, attacker calls emergencyWithdraw(token, 1) with 0 balance
Verify balance wraps to near-max uint128
Verify attacker can then call withdraw() to drain remaining tokens

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Victim deposits 1000 USDC. 2. Attacker (0 balance) calls emergencyWithdraw(USDC, 1). 3. _processWithdrawal: unchecked subtraction wraps attacker balance to ~3.4e38, transfers 1 USDC. 4. Attacker calls withdraw(USDC, 999). Balance check passes (3.4e38 >= 999). 5. All remaining USDC drained. Total stolen: 1000 USDC.
prerequisites: Any tokens deposited by other users

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of ALL deposited tokens for every token type
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - funds irrecoverably stolen, no admin/pause mechanism

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for emergencyWithdraw with amount exceeding balance

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Two transactions in sequence, no timing constraint

validation_result.impact_summary: Any user with zero balance can drain all deposited tokens from the contract by exploiting an unchecked arithmetic underflow in emergencyWithdraw/_processWithdrawal, wrapping their balance to ~2^128-1 and then withdrawing all contract funds via withdraw().
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The emergencyWithdraw function is external with zero validation: no balance check, no zero-amount check, no zero-address check, and no access control. It directly calls _processWithdrawal which performs balance subtraction inside an unchecked block. When a user with 0 balance calls emergencyWithdraw(token, 1), the unchecked subtraction `balances[user][token] -= amount` wraps from 0 to type(uint128).max (340282366920938463463374607431768211455). The safeTransfer of 1 token succeeds because the contract holds tokens from other depositors. The attacker's internal balance is now astronomically large and they can call withdraw() which properly checks `balances[msg.sender][token] < amount` — this check passes for any amount up to type(uint128).max. The attacker drains all remaining tokens.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. Lines in _processWithdrawal show the unchecked block performing `balances[user][token] -= amount` and `totalDeposits[token] -= amount` without any prior balance validation. emergencyWithdraw calls _processWithdrawal with no guards whatsoever. The withdraw function has a balance sufficiency check that would pass after the underflow since the attacker's balance is near-max uint128.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No balance check, no zero-amount check, no zero-address check, no access control. Any user can call with any amount for any token. Directly invokes _processWithdrawal which has unchecked arithmetic.

The unchecked block allows uint128 underflow on balances[user][token]. If user has 0 balance and amount is 1, balance wraps to type(uint128).max. The safeTransfer succeeds if the contract holds sufficient tokens from other depositors.

After the underflow exploit, the attacker's balance is type(uint128).max. The balance check `balances[msg.sender][token] < amount` passes for any reasonable withdrawal amount, enabling the attacker to drain all remaining contract tokens.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls emergencyWithdraw(tokenAddress, 1) with 0 balance for that token
3. In _processWithdrawal, unchecked { balances[attacker][token] -= 1 } wraps 0 to type(uint128).max
4. unchecked { totalDeposits[token] -= 1 } reduces totalDeposits by 1 (no underflow if other deposits exist)
5. IERC20(token).safeTransfer(attacker, 1) succeeds — contract holds tokens from other depositors
6. Attacker now has internal balance of type(uint128).max (~3.4e38)
8. withdraw checks: balances[attacker][token] < amount → type(uint128).max < remainingBalance → false → passes
9. safeTransfer sends all remaining tokens to attacker
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: emergencyWithdraw(token, 1) → _processWithdrawal(msg.sender, token, 1) → unchecked { balances[msg.sender][token] -= 1 } (underflows from 0 to 2^128-1) → safeTransfer(msg.sender, 1) succeeds → attacker balance is now ~3.4e38 → withdraw(token, X) where X is remaining contract balance → balance check passes → all funds drained

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The vulnerability exists in the contract code itself regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is fully validated. Step-by-step: (1) Any legitimate user deposits tokens into the contract via deposit(). (2) Attacker with zero balance calls emergencyWithdraw(token, 1). There are no guards in emergencyWithdraw — no balance check, no zero checks. (3) _processWithdrawal executes `unchecked { balances[attacker][token] -= 1; }` which wraps uint128 from 0 to 340282366920938463463374607431768211455. (4) safeTransfer sends 1 token to attacker (succeeds because contract holds depositor tokens). (5) Attacker calls withdraw(token, contractBalance-1). The check `balances[msg.sender][token] < amount` evaluates to `3.4e38 < contractBalance` which is false, so it passes. (6) All remaining tokens transferred to attacker. Attack cost: gas for 2 transactions (~100K gas). Capital required: zero.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Complete loss of ALL deposited tokens for every token type in the contract. Any address can exploit this with zero capital. No admin/pause mechanism exists to prevent or mitigate the attack. All depositors lose all funds with no recovery possibility. The vulnerability is trivially exploitable and can be automated to drain all token types in rapid succession.
recommended_mitigations: Add balance validation to emergencyWithdraw: require(balances[msg.sender][token] >= amount, 'InsufficientBalance')
Remove the unchecked block from _processWithdrawal, or add explicit balance checks before the unchecked arithmetic
Add zero-address and zero-amount checks to emergencyWithdraw matching those in withdraw()
Consider removing emergencyWithdraw entirely and using the already-validated withdraw() function
If emergencyWithdraw must exist, make it call the same validated path as withdraw() or share a common internal function with proper checks
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy StablecoinDEX and a mock ERC20 token. 2. Have victim deposit 1000e6 tokens via deposit(). 3. From attacker address (0 balance), call emergencyWithdraw(token, 1). 4. Assert attacker's internal balance equals type(uint128).max. 5. Call withdraw(token, 999e6) from attacker. 6. Assert contract token balance is now ~0 and attacker received 1000e6 tokens total.
additional_testing_needed: Verify the totalDeposits underflow behavior when multiple tokens are attacked simultaneously
Test whether totalDeposits underflow (uint256) after emergency withdraw affects subsequent withdraw() calls for other users
